Method: The authors propose a 'simulation-in-the-loop' interaction paradigm, where users and agents collaboratively explore simulated trajectories of future actions to make better-informed decisions.

Result: The paper provides a conceptual framework for simulation-based collaboration and demonstrates its potential effectiveness through illustrative human-agent collaboration scenarios.

Conclusion: Simulation-based methods can transform human-agent interaction into a more informed and effective process by introducing foresight into decision-making, enabling better control and discovery of constraints and preferences.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly used to power autonomous agents for complex, multi-step tasks. However, human-agent interaction remains pointwise and reactive: users approve or correct individual actions to mitigate immediate risks, without visibility into subsequent consequences. This forces users to mentally simulate long-term effects, a cognitively demanding and often inaccurate process. Users have control over individual steps but lack the foresight to make informed decisions. We argue that effective collaboration requires foresight, not just control. We propose simulation-in-the-loop, an interaction paradigm that enables users and agents to explore simulated future trajectories before committing to decisions. Simulation transforms intervention from reactive guesswork into informed exploration, while helping users discover latent constraints and preferences along the way. This perspective paper characterizes the limitations of current paradigms, introduces a conceptual framework for simulation-based collaboration, and illustrates its potential through concrete human-agent collaboration scenarios.

</details>


### [479] [Human-Centred LLM Privacy Audits: Findings and Frictions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.12094)
*Dimitri Staufer,Kirsten Morehouse,David Hartmann,Bettina Berendt*

Main category: cs.HC

TL;DR: This paper develops a self-audit tool (LMP2) to allow individuals to inspect their associations embedded in language models like GPT-4, identifying challenges in auditing and providing recommendations for improving privacy evaluations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper is motivated by the lack of practical tools for individuals to understand and control statistical associations that large language models (LLMs) make about them, raising privacy and interpretability concerns.

Method: The authors conducted two user studies involving 458 participants to explore model associations (using GPT-4) and examined name-conditioned predictions through their designed browser-based tool, LMP2. They also validated their probing method across multiple LLMs using public figures and non-existent names.

Result: The study found that GPT-4 can predict some personal features (11 out of 50) with at least 60% accuracy and identified significant complexities in developing robust evaluation methods for LLM-generated associations.

Conclusion: To enable actionable and reliable privacy audits for LLMs, the authors highlight nine challenges encountered during the study and propose future directions for creating human-centered audit systems that address current operational challenges.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) learn statistical associations from massive training corpora and user interactions, and deployed systems can surface or infer information about individuals. Yet people lack practical ways to inspect what a model associates with their name. We report interim findings from an ongoing study and introduce LMP2, a browser-based self-audit tool. In two user studies ($N_{total}{=}458$), GPT-4o predicts 11 of 50 features for everyday people with $\ge$60\% accuracy, and participants report wanting control over LLM-generated associations despite not considering all outputs privacy violations. To validate our probing method, we evaluate eight LLMs on public figures and non-existent names, observing clear separation between stable name-conditioned associations and model defaults. Our findings also contribute to exposing a broader generative AI evaluation crisis: when outputs are probabilistic, context-dependent, and user-mediated through elicitation, what model--individual associations even include is under-specified and operationalisation relies on crafting probes and metrics that are hard to validate or compare. To move towards reliable, actionable human-centred LLM privacy audits, we identify nine frictions that emerged in our study and offer recommendations for future work and the design of human-centred LLM privacy audits.

</details>


### [480] ["I followed what felt right, not what I was told": Autonomy, Coaching, and Recognizing Bias Through AI-Mediated Dialogue](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11274)
*Atieh Taheri,Hamza El Alaoui,Patrick Carrington,Jeffrey P. Bigham*

Main category: cs.HC

TL;DR: This paper explores how AI-mediated dialogue can help people recognize ableist microaggressions, comparing different intervention approaches.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is a lack of effective interventions to help people identify ableist microaggressions despite their continued prevalence in daily life.

Method: An experiment with 160 participants tested AI-mediated dialogue across four conditions (Bias-Directed, Neutral-Directed, Self-Directed, and Reading). Participants rated scenarios, provided reflections, and outcomes were analyzed quantitatively and qualitatively.

Result: Dialogue-based interventions improved recognition of ableist microaggressions better than reading alone, with Bias-Directed approaches increasing negativity but sharpening discernment of bias, and Neutral-Directed approaches showing balance.

Conclusion: AI-mediated dialogue can enhance recognition of ableism, though trade-offs exist in how conversational systems employ bias-related interventions.

Abstract: Ableist microaggressions remain pervasive in everyday interactions, yet interventions to help people recognize them are limited. We present an experiment testing how AI-mediated dialogue influences recognition of ableism. 160 participants completed a pre-test, intervention, and a post-test across four conditions: AI nudges toward bias (Bias-Directed), inclusion (Neutral-Directed), unguided dialogue (Self-Directed), and a text-only non-dialogue (Reading). Participants rated scenarios on standardness of social experience and emotional impact; those in dialogue-based conditions also provided qualitative reflections. Quantitative results showed dialogue-based conditions produced stronger recognition than Reading, though trajectories diverged: biased nudges improved differentiation of bias from neutrality but increased overall negativity. Inclusive or no nudges remained more balanced, while Reading participants showed weaker gains and even declines. Qualitative findings revealed biased nudges were often rejected, while inclusive nudges were adopted as scaffolding. We contribute a validated vignette corpus, an AI-mediated intervention platform, and design implications highlighting trade-offs conversational systems face when integrating bias-related nudges.

</details>


### [481] [Ghost Framing Theory: Exploring the role of generative AI in new venture rhetorical legitimation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11384)
*Greg Nyilasy*

Main category: cs.HC

TL;DR: The paper introduces Ghost Framing Theory (GFT) to explain how AI-human collaboration affects entrepreneurial framing and legitimacy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the growing but invisible role of generative AI (genAI) in entrepreneurial framing and rhetorical legitimation.

Method: Developing Ghost Framing Theory, identifying genAI rhetorical affordances, and proposing a process model for how resonance and legitimacy are co-produced.

Result: The research presents a novel theoretical framework and expands understanding of genAI's role in entrepreneurial contexts through framing, legitimacy, and affordance theories.

Conclusion: Ghost Framing Theory offers new insights into human-AI collaborations, highlighting their impact on entrepreneurship and the emergence of resonance dynamics.

Abstract: Responding to the surging but largely invisible use of generative AI in entrepreneurial framing, I advance Ghost Framing Theory (GFT) to explain how hybrid founder- and investor-genAI ensembles co-produce, contest, and recalibrate resonance in the rhetorical legitimation of new ventures. Building on scholarship in framing, micro-level legitimacy judgments, and sociomaterial affordances, I identify genAI rhetorical affordances (generativeness, extreme combinatorics, tone repertoire, velocity/energy and shared substratum) and theorize a recursive/iterative process model (ghost pitching, ghost screening, ghost relationship-building), configuring emergent resonance and legitimation. GFT builds new rhetorical framing theory for the age of genAI, connects research on human-AI collaboration with cultural entrepreneurship and extends affordance theory into multi-actor scenarios where affordance transitivity and visibility emerge as key considerations.

</details>


### [482] [Evaluation format, not model capability, drives triage failure in the assessment of consumer health AI](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11413)
*David Fraile Navarro,Farah Magrabi,Enrico Coiera*

Main category: cs.HC

TL;DR: The paper re-evaluated ChatGPT Health's triage performance, highlighting the flaws of previous 'exam-style' evaluation methods and advocating for assessments that reflect real-world interactions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing AI triage safety critiques (e.g., ChatGPT under-triaging 51.6% of emergencies) were based on artificial test conditions that don't mirror actual consumer use.

Method: Five frontier LLMs were tested on health scenarios under both constrained (exam-style) and naturalistic (real-world) conditions, including analyzing model behavior with varied input prompts.

Result: Naturalistic interaction improved triage accuracy by 6.4%. Diabetic ketoacidosis was triaged correctly 100% of the time across all configurations, and free-text responses showed major improvements over forced-choice formats for emergency care recommendations.

Conclusion: Evaluation frameworks need to reflect real-world use to obtain valid assessments of AI in healthcare, as forced, artificial conditions lead to inaccurate conclusions about their safety and performance.

Abstract: Ramaswamy et al. reported in \textit{Nature Medicine} that ChatGPT Health under-triages 51.6\% of emergencies, concluding that consumer-facing AI triage poses safety risks. However, their evaluation used an exam-style protocol -- forced A/B/C/D output, knowledge suppression, and suppression of clarifying questions -- that differs fundamentally from how consumers use health chatbots. We tested five frontier LLMs (GPT-5.2, Claude Sonnet 4.6, Claude Opus 4.6, Gemini 3 Flash, Gemini 3.1 Pro) on a 17-scenario partial replication bank under constrained (exam-style, 1,275 trials) and naturalistic (patient-style messages, 850 trials) conditions, with targeted ablations and prompt-faithful checks using the authors' released prompts. Naturalistic interaction improved triage accuracy by 6.4 percentage points ($p = 0.015$). Diabetic ketoacidosis was correctly triaged in 100\% of trials across all models and conditions. Asthma triage improved from 48\% to 80\%. The forced A/B/C/D format was the dominant failure mechanism: three models scored 0--24\% with forced choice but 100\% with free text (all $p < 10^{-8}$), consistently recommending emergency care in their own words while the forced-choice format registered under-triage. Prompt-faithful checks on the authors' exact released prompts confirmed the scaffold produces model-dependent, case-dependent results. The headline under-triage rate is highly contingent on evaluation format and should not be interpreted as a stable estimate of deployed triage behavior. Valid evaluation of consumer health AI requires testing under conditions that reflect actual use.

</details>


### [483] [From Pen Strokes to Sleep States: Detecting Low-Recovery Days Using Sigma-Lognormal Handwriting Features](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11512)
*Chisa Tanaka,Andrew Vargo,Anna Scius-Bertrand,Andreas Fischer,Koichi Kise*

Main category: cs.HC

TL;DR: This paper explores how variations in sleep-related physiological recovery states can be detected through online handwriting dynamics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The study aims to uncover whether handwriting can reflect day-to-day physiological fluctuations in healthy individuals, as existing research mainly focuses on disease classification and character recognition.

Method: The authors used a personalized binary classification framework driven by the Sigma-Lognormal model for analyzing handwriting, conducted a 28-day study across 13 participants, and utilized wearable devices to collect nocturnal cardiac and sleep data.

Result: The classification for identifying low-recovery days showed significantly higher performance than the baseline, especially for cardiac-related sleep metrics, with no significant effect of task type or recording times.

Conclusion: Day-to-day variations in autonomic recovery states can be detected non-invasively from handwriting, providing a device-independent health monitoring approach.

Abstract: While handwriting has traditionally been studied for character recognition and disease classification, its potential to reflect day-to-day physiological fluctuations in healthy individuals remains unexplored. This study examines whether daily variations in sleep-related recovery states can be inferred from online handwriting dynamics. %
We propose a personalized binary classification framework that detects low-recovery days using features derived from the Sigma-Lognormal model, which captures the neuromotor generation process of pen strokes. In a 28-day in-the-wild study involving 13 university students, handwriting was recorded three times daily, and nocturnal cardiac indicators were measured using a wearable ring. For each participant, the lowest (or highest) quartile of four sleep-related metrics -- HRV, lowest heart rate, average heart rate, and total sleep duration -- defined the positive class. Leave-One-Day-Out cross-validation showed that PR-AUC significantly exceeded the baseline (0.25) for all four variables after FDR correction, with the strongest performance observed for cardiac-related variables. Importantly, classification performance did not differ significantly across task types or recording timings, indicating that recovery-related signals are embedded in general movement dynamics. These results demonstrate that subtle within-person autonomic recovery fluctuations can be detected from everyday handwriting, opening a new direction for non-invasive, device-independent health monitoring.

</details>


### [484] [Prediction of Grade, Gender, and Academic Performance of Children and Teenagers from Handwriting Using the Sigma-Lognormal Model](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11519)
*Adrian Iste,Kazuki Nishizawa,Chisa Tanaka,Andrew Vargo,Anna Scius-Bertrand,Andreas Fischer,Koichi Kise*

Main category: cs.HC

TL;DR: This paper investigates handwriting dynamics of Japanese students using kinematic features to predict grade, gender, and academic performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To explore the underutilized potential of handwriting analysis in studying developmental and educational characteristics in children.

Method: Analyzed a large-scale dataset with three feature sets (kinematic descriptors, entropy-based measures, sigma-lognormal model parameters) aggregated at the student level for prediction tasks using regression and Random Forest models.

Result: Handwriting dynamics successfully predicted grade, gender, and academic performance, indicating measurable developmental and individual differences, particularly for grade prediction.

Conclusion: Children's handwriting reflects developmental stages and evolves toward lognormal motor organization, confirming its analytical potential in educational settings.

Abstract: Digital handwriting acquisition enables the capture of detailed temporal and kinematic signals reflecting the motor processes underlying writing behavior. While handwriting analysis has been extensively explored in clinical or adult populations, its potential for studying developmental and educational characteristics in children remains less investigated. In this work, we examine whether handwriting dynamics encode information related to student characteristics using a large-scale online dataset collected from Japanese students from elementary school to junior high school. We systematically compare three families of handwriting-derived features: basic statistical descriptors of kinematic signals, entropy-based measures of variability, and parameters obtained from the sigma-lognormal model. Although the dataset contains dense stroke-level recordings, features are aggregated at the student level to enable a controlled comparison between representations. These features are evaluated across three prediction tasks: grade prediction, gender classification, and academic performance classification, using Linear or Logistic Regression and Random Forest models under consistent experimental settings. The results show that handwriting dynamics contain measurable signals related to developmental stage and individual differences, especially for the grade prediction task. These findings highlight the potential of kinematic handwriting analysis and confirm that through their development, children's handwriting evolves toward a lognormal motor organization.

</details>


### [485] [Shadowless Projection Mapping for Tabletop Workspaces with Synthetic Aperture Projector](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11551)
*Takahiro Okamoto,Masaki Takeuchi,Masataka Sawayama,Daisuke Iwai*

Main category: cs.HC

TL;DR: This paper introduces a synthetic-aperture projection mapping (PM) system enabling augmented reality (AR) in tabletop workspaces using dense projectors, eliminating shadows and latency issues, and minimizing sense of projection (SoP) through user studies.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Conventional projection mapping systems suffer from issues like projection shadows and latency in multi-user tabletop workspaces, hindering AR experiences. The study aims to address these limitations for broader applications such as industrial design, healthcare, and urban planning.

Method: The authors propose a synthetic-aperture PM system leveraging densely arranged projectors, avoiding computational compensation to eliminate shadows and latency. They further develop an offline blur compensation method to address subpixel misalignment without affecting spatial resolution beyond the environment setup.

Result: The system achieves delay-free, shadowless projections without degrading resolution, while the proposed framework minimizes perceptual impression (SoP). User studies validate the efficacy of reducing the SoP in altering material properties seamlessly.

Conclusion: This novel PM design framework enhances AR experiences in multi-user tabletop environments by addressing fundamental user-experience issues such as shadows, latency, and SoP, making it valuable for diverse applications like collaboration, design, and planning.

Abstract: Projection mapping (PM) enables augmented reality (AR) experiences without requiring users to wear head-mounted displays and supports multi-user interaction. It is regarded as a promising technology for a variety of applications in which users interact with content superimposed onto augmented objects in tabletop workspaces, including remote collaboration, healthcare, industrial design, urban planning, artwork creation, and office work. However, conventional PM systems often suffer from projection shadows when users occlude the light path. Prior approaches employing multiple distributed projectors can compensate for occlusion, but suffer from latency due to computational processing, degrading the user experience. In this research, we introduce a synthetic-aperture PM system that uses a significantly larger number of projectors, arranged densely in the environment, to achieve delay-free, shadowless projection for tabletop workspaces without requiring computational compensation. To address spatial resolution degradation caused by subpixel misalignment among overlaid projections, we develop and validate an offline blur compensation method whose computation time remains independent of the number of projectors. Furthermore, we demonstrate that our shadowless PM plays a critical role in achieving a fundamental goal of PM: altering material properties without evoking projection-like impression. Specifically, we define this perceptual impression as ``sense of projection (SoP)'' and establish a PM design framework to minimize the SoP based on user studies.

</details>


### [486] [Modeling Trial-and-Error Navigation With a Sequential Decision Model of Information Scent](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11759)
*Xiaofu Jin,Yunpeng Bai,Antti Oulasvirta*

Main category: cs.HC

TL;DR: The paper investigates user navigation behavior in information architectures and introduces an improved model of information scent that accounts for sequential decision-making under memory constraints.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Understand user struggles in navigating complex information architectures and explain errors in link selection.

Method: Developed a model where navigation is framed as a sequential decision-making problem. The model accounts for memory constraints and partial inspection behavior.

Result: The model accurately replicates observed user behaviors like premature link selection, wrong turns, and backtracking recovery.

Conclusion: The updated concept of information scent successfully explains users' navigation decisions, considering memory constraints and stepwise decision-making.

Abstract: Users often struggle to locate an item within an information architecture, particularly when links are ambiguous or deeply nested in hierarchies. Information scent has been used to explain why users select incorrect links, but this concept assumes that users see all available links before deciding. In practice, users frequently select a link too quickly, overlook relevant cues, and then rely on backtracking when errors occur. We extend the concept of information scent by framing navigation as a sequential decision-making problem under memory constraints. Specifically, we assume that users do not scan entire pages but instead inspect strategically, looking "just enough" to find the target given their time budget. To choose which item to inspect next, they consider both local (this page) and global (site) scent; however, both are constrained by memory. Trying to avoid wasting time, they occasionally choose the wrong links without inspecting everything on a page. Comparisons with empirical data show that our model replicates key navigation behaviors: premature selections, wrong turns, and recovery from backtracking. We conclude that trial-and-error behavior is well explained by information scent when accounting for the sequential and bounded characteristics of the navigation problem.

</details>


### [487] [An Intent of Collaboration: On Agencies between Designers and Emerging (Intelligent) Technologies](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.12018)
*Pei-Ying Lin,Julie Heij,Iris Borst,Britt Joosten,Kristina Andersen,Wijnand IJsselsteijn*

Main category: cs.HC

TL;DR: This paper investigates how designers can retain creative agency when working with Google's LLM by reflecting on their creative process and understanding human-technology dynamics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Designers increasingly work with generative AIs like LLMs but face challenges in maintaining creativity and agency due to the AI’s limitations and foreign nature.

Method: Three designers engaged in a three-month exploratory project co-creating with Google's LLM to study its impact on their creativity.

Result: The study revealed a power dynamic where designers can lose their creative agency, but introspecting their creative methods and understanding the technology can help regain it.

Conclusion: To thrive with emerging intelligent technologies, designers should focus on their creative cognitive activities, critically assess the technology’s capabilities, and adjust their partnership dynamics with the technology.

Abstract: Amidst the emergence of powerful intelligent technologies such as LLMs and text-to-image AIs that promise to enhance creative processes, designers face the challenges of remaining empowered and creative while working with these foreign digital partners. While generative AIs offer versatile, informative, and occasionally poetic outcomes, their lack of embodied knowledge presents an even greater challenge to designers in gaining fruitful outcomes, such as in the field of Digital Craftsmanship. In this project, three designers embarked on a three-month experimental journey with an intention to co-create with Google's LLM as a potential intelligent partner to investigate how it will influence the designers' creativity. We found that a power dynamic of agencies exists between the LLM and the designer, in which the designer can easily lose their creative agency. Regaining the designer's creative agency involves introspection into their own creative process, a structural understanding of the specific emerging technology involved, and deliberate adjustments to the dynamics of the human-technology relationship. We propose paying attention to the designer's inner world and parties of agencies when engaging with emerging intelligent technologies through three aspects: the sensitivity towards a creative process as cognitive activities; the active investigation into specific technology's capability; and the adjustment towards an appropriate working relationship between the designer and the emerging technology.

</details>


<div id='physics.med-ph'></div>

# physics.med-ph [[Back]](#toc)

### [488] [MRI2Qmap: multi-parametric quantitative mapping with MRI-driven denoising priors](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11316)
*Mohammad Golbabaee,Matteo Cencini,Carolin Pirkl,Marion Menzel,Michela Tosetti,Bjoern Menze*

Main category: physics.med-ph

TL;DR: MRI2Qmap is a framework for quantitative MRI reconstruction using priors from routine weighted-MRI datasets, bypassing the need for specific quantitative imaging training data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome the scarcity of quantitative imaging data for training in MRF-type sequences, enabling better image reconstruction without aliasing artifacts.

Method: MRI2Qmap integrates a physical acquisition model with spatial priors learned using deep denoising autoencoders pretrained on weighted-MRI datasets.

Result: MRI2Qmap achieves improved retention of accuracy and artifact reduction in 3D whole-brain MRF data, validating its efficacy with in-vivo and simulated acquisitions.

Conclusion: MRI2Qmap provides a scalable quantitative reconstruction approach leveraging routine clinical MRI datasets, supporting broader applications of accelerated MRF techniques.

Abstract: Magnetic Resonance Fingerprinting (MRF) and other highly accelerated transient-state parameter mapping techniques enable simultaneous quantification of multiple tissue properties, but often suffer from aliasing artifacts due to compressed sampling. Incorporating spatial image priors can mitigate these artifacts, and deep learning has shown strong potential when large training datasets are available. However, extending this paradigm to MRF-type sequences remains challenging due to the scarcity of quantitative imaging data for training. Can this limitation be overcome by leveraging sources of training data from clinically-routine weighted MRI images? To this end, we introduce MRI2Qmap, a plug-and-play quantitative reconstruction framework that integrates the physical acquisition model with priors learned from deep denoising autoencoders pretrained on large multimodal weighted-MRI datasets. MRI2Qmap demonstrates that spatial-domain structural priors learned from independently acquired datasets of routine weighted-MRI images can be effectively used for quantitative MRI reconstruction. The proposed method is validated on highly accelerated 3D whole-brain MRF data from both in-vivo and simulated acquisitions, achieving competitive or superior performance relative to existing baselines without requiring ground-truth quantitative imaging data for training. By decoupling quantitative reconstruction from the need for ground-truth MRF training data, this framework points toward a scalable paradigm for quantitative MRI that can capitalize on the large and growing repositories of routine clinical MRI.

</details>


<div id='math.ST'></div>

# math.ST [[Back]](#toc)

### [489] [Conformal e-prediction in the presence of confounding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11134)
*Vladimir Vovk,Ruodu Wang*

Main category: math.ST

TL;DR: The research expands conformal e-prediction methods to address observed confounding between data and labels.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The study aims to enhance conformal prediction models to incorporate scenarios where confounding variables may influence predictions.

Method: The authors analyze both IID cases and scenarios with dependence between observations, integrating confounded relationships into conformal e-prediction.

Result: The paper provides a theoretical foundation for conformal e-prediction in cases of observed confounding.

Conclusion: This work broadens the applicability of conformal e-prediction, making it usable in more complex data scenarios with confounding factors.

Abstract: This note extends conformal e-prediction to cover the case where there is observed confounding between the random object $X$ and its label $Y$. We consider both the case where the observed data is IID and a case where some dependence between observations is permitted.

</details>


<div id='math.NA'></div>

# math.NA [[Back]](#toc)

### [490] [A Machine Learning-Enhanced Hopf-Cole Formulation for Nonlinear Gas Flow in Porous Media](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11250)
*V. S. Maduru,K. B. Nakshatrala*

Main category: math.NA

TL;DR: This paper develops a robust, accurate, and efficient framework for modeling gas transport in porous media using advanced mathematical transformation, shared-trunk neural networks, and a Deep Least-Squares solver.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve accurate modeling of gas flow in porous media, addressing challenges caused by nonlinear behavior and parameter uncertainty, including those induced by pressure-dependent permeability.

Method: The framework integrates a Hopf-Cole transformation to linearize equations, a mixed-formulation with a shared-trunk neural network for pressure-velocity prediction, and a Deep Least-Squares solver for enhanced performance.

Result: Numerical results demonstrate the framework's ability to accurately model flow dynamics and estimate parameters across different pressure regimes, showing robustness, accuracy, and efficiency.

Conclusion: The proposed method significantly advances gas transport modeling in porous media, enabling effective simulations and parameter inversions even under complex conditions.

Abstract: Accurate modeling of gas flow through porous media is critical for many technological applications, including reservoir performance prediction, carbon capture and sequestration, and fuel cells and batteries. However, such modeling remains challenging due to strong nonlinear behavior and uncertainty in model parameters. In particular, gas slippage effects described by the Klinkenberg model introduce pressure-dependent permeability, which complicates numerical simulation and obscures deviations from classical Darcy flow behavior. To address these challenges, we present an integrated modeling framework for gas transport in porous media that combines a Klinkenberg-enhanced constitutive relation, Hopf-Cole-transformed mixed-form linear governing equations, a shared-trunk neural network architecture, and a Deep Least-Squares (DeepLS) solver. The Hopf-Cole transformation reformulates the original nonlinear flow equations into an equivalent linear system closely related to the Darcy model, while the mixed formulation, together with a shared-trunk neural architecture, enables simultaneous and accurate prediction of both pressure and velocity fields. A rigorous convergence analysis is performed both theoretically and numerically, establishing the stability and convergence properties of the proposed solver. Importantly, the proposed framework also naturally facilitates inverse modeling of pressure-dependent permeability and slippage parameters from limited or indirect observations, enabling efficient estimation of flow properties that are difficult to measure experimentally. Numerical results demonstrate accurate recovery of flow dynamics and parameters across a wide range of pressure regimes, highlighting the framework's robustness, accuracy, and computational efficiency for gas transport modeling and inversion in tight formations.

</details>


<div id='cs.SD'></div>

# cs.SD [[Back]](#toc)

### [491] [Uni-ASR: Unified LLM-Based Architecture for Non-Streaming and Streaming Automatic Speech Recognition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11123)
*Yinfeng Xia,Jian Tang,Junfeng Hou,Gaopeng Xu,Haitao Yao*

Main category: cs.SD

TL;DR: This paper introduces Uni-ASR, a unified LLM-based framework tackling both streaming and non-streaming ASR with promising accuracy under latency constraints.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enhance low-latency streaming ASR performance while maintaining the accuracy of non-streaming modes.

Method: Propose Uni-ASR, a joint training paradigm enabling seamless transitions between modes, coupled with context-aware training and fallback decoding strategies.

Result: Uni-ASR achieves competitive accuracy in non-streaming and improves accuracy in streaming scenarios under varied latency conditions.

Conclusion: Uni-ASR effectively integrates LLMs for versatile ASR capabilities, enhancing streaming performance without adding latency.

Abstract: Although the deep integration of the Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR) system with Large Language Models (LLMs) has significantly improved accuracy, the deployment of such systems in low-latency streaming scenarios remains challenging. In this paper, we propose Uni-ASR, a unified framework based on LLMs that integrates both non-streaming and streaming speech recognition capabilities. We propose a joint training paradigm that enables the system to seamlessly transition between two recognition modes without any architectural modifications. Furthermore, we introduce a context-aware training paradigm and a co-designed fallback decoding strategy, which can enhance streaming recognition accuracy without introducing additional latency. The experimental results demonstrate that Uni-ASR not only achieves competitive performance within non-streaming mode, but also demonstrates strong effectiveness in streaming scenarios under diverse latency constraints.

</details>


### [492] [AnimeScore: A Preference-Based Dataset and Framework for Evaluating Anime-Like Speech Style](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11482)
*Joonyong Park,Jerry Li*

Main category: cs.SD

TL;DR: The paper proposes AnimeScore, an objective framework for evaluating 'anime-like' voices using pairwise ranking instead of subjective judgments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Anime-likeness in voices lacks a standardized objective metric, making existing subjective evaluations costly and inconsistent.

Method: The authors created a preference-based evaluation framework called AnimeScore, collecting 15,000 pairwise judgments and analyzing acoustic features.

Result: The study discovered key characteristics underpinning anime-likeness and demonstrated that SSL-based ranking models achieve an AUC of up to 90.8%, providing a reliable metric.

Conclusion: AnimeScore offers a standard, objective method to quantitatively evaluate anime-like voices, facilitating future optimizations in generative speech models.

Abstract: Evaluating 'anime-like' voices currently relies on costly subjective judgments, yet no standardized objective metric exists. A key challenge is that anime-likeness, unlike naturalness, lacks a shared absolute scale, making conventional Mean Opinion Score (MOS) protocols unreliable. To address this gap, we propose AnimeScore, a preference-based framework for automatic anime-likeness evaluation via pairwise ranking. We collect 15,000 pairwise judgments from 187 evaluators with free-form descriptions, and acoustic analysis reveals that perceived anime-likeness is driven by controlled resonance shaping, prosodic continuity, and deliberate articulation rather than simple heuristics such as high pitch. We show that handcrafted acoustic features reach a 69.3% AUC ceiling, while SSL-based ranking models achieve up to 90.8% AUC, providing a practical metric that can also serve as a reward signal for preference-based optimization of generative speech models.

</details>


### [493] [Resurfacing Paralinguistic Awareness in Large Audio Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11947)
*Hao Yang,Minghan Wang,Tongtong Wu,Lizhen Qu,Ehsan Shareghi,Gholamreza Haffari*

Main category: cs.SD

TL;DR: The paper proposes fine-tuning techniques to enable Large Audio Language Models (LALMs) to better understand paralinguistic cues, enhancing their interaction capabilities.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LALMs miss out on utilizing paralinguistic cues (pitch, tone, emotions), which are rich in user context, as they currently rely solely on content-based responses.

Method: Five systematic analyses identify layers in LALMs responsible for semantics and paralinguistic understanding. A selective fine-tuning protocol (PE-FT) is introduced, including selective-layer fine-tuning and a dual-level classification head.

Result: The PE-FT protocol improves LALMs' sensitivity to paralinguistic cues and outperforms traditional all-layer fine-tuning strategies.

Conclusion: By integrating selective layer fine-tuning and auxiliary classification, LALMs can effectively incorporate paralinguistic awareness, leading to more contextually aware and interactive performance.

Abstract: Large Audio Language Models (LALMs) have expanded the interaction with human to speech modality, which introduces great interactive potential, due to the paralinguistic cues implicitly indicating the user context. However, building on the current content-centred paradigm, LALMs usually neglect such paralinguistic cues and respond solely based on query content. In this work, to resurface the paralinguistic awareness in LALMs, we introduce five diverse layer-wise analyses to jointly identify paralinguistic layers and semantic understanding layers. Based on these insights, we propose a paralinguistic-enhanced fine-tuning (PE-FT) protocol accordingly to equip LALMs with paralinguistic-aware capabilities, including (1) selective-layer fine-tuning, and (2) an auxiliary dual-level classification head. Our experiments demonstrate that PE-FT protocol efficiently and effectively resurfaces the paralinguistic awareness, even surpassing the performance of the all-layer fine-tuning strategy.

</details>


### [494] [Toward Complex-Valued Neural Networks for Waveform Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11589)
*Hyung-Seok Oh,Deok-Hyeon Cho,Seung-Bin Kim,Seong-Whan Lee*

Main category: cs.SD

TL;DR: The paper introduces ComVo, a complex-valued neural vocoder that improves waveform generation quality and training efficiency, outperforming real-valued baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome the limitations of real-valued vocoders that process real and imaginary spectrogram components independently, reducing their ability to capture structured spectrogram complexities.

Method: ComVo employs native complex arithmetic in its generator and discriminator, uses phase quantization for structured phase transformations, and introduces block-matrix computation for efficient training.

Result: ComVo achieves higher synthesis quality compared to real-valued vocoders and reduces training time by 25% with block-matrix computation.

Conclusion: ComVo offers a more efficient and higher-quality approach to neural vocoder-based waveform synthesis and provides a promising direction for leveraging native complex-valued computations in deep learning.

Abstract: Neural vocoders have recently advanced waveform generation, yielding natural and expressive audio. Among these approaches, iSTFT-based vocoders have recently gained attention. They predict a complex-valued spectrogram and then synthesize the waveform via iSTFT, thereby avoiding learned upsampling stages that can increase computational cost. However, current approaches use real-valued networks that process the real and imaginary parts independently. This separation limits their ability to capture the inherent structure of complex spectrograms. We present ComVo, a Complex-valued neural Vocoder whose generator and discriminator use native complex arithmetic. This enables an adversarial training framework that provides structured feedback in complex-valued representations. To guide phase transformations in a structured manner, we introduce phase quantization, which discretizes phase values and regularizes the training process. Finally, we propose a block-matrix computation scheme to improve training efficiency by reducing redundant operations. Experiments demonstrate that ComVo achieves higher synthesis quality than comparable real-valued baselines, and that its block-matrix scheme reduces training time by 25%. Audio samples and code are available at https://hs-oh-prml.github.io/ComVo/.

</details>


### [495] [Continued Pretraining for Low-Resource Swahili ASR: Achieving State-of-the-Art Performance with Minimal Labeled Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11378)
*Hillary Mutisya,John Mugane*

Main category: cs.SD

TL;DR: The paper improves Swahili speech recognition using continued pretraining (CPT) with unlabeled and limited labeled audio data, achieving significant performance gains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The authors aim to advance automatic speech recognition (ASR) for Swahili, a low-resource language, by leveraging limited labeled data and unlabeled audio.

Method: The approach involves pseudo-labeled continued pretraining (CPT) combined with supervised fine-tuning, using wav2vec2-bert-2.0 for ASR.

Result: With 20,000 labeled samples, the method achieves a word error rate (WER) of 3.24% for Common Voice Swahili, representing an 82% improvement over the baseline and surpassing the previous best academic system by 61%.

Conclusion: This methodology demonstrates effectiveness in handling low-resource languages, suggesting its replicability for other similar scenarios.

Abstract: We investigate continued pretraining (CPT) for adapting wav2vec2-bert-2.0 to Swahili automatic speech recognition (ASR). Our approach combines unlabeled audio with limited labeled data through pseudo-labeled CPT followed by supervised finetuning. With 20,000 labeled samples, we achieve 3.24% WER on Common Voice Swahili-an 82% relative improvement over the baseline. This result surpasses the best previously reported academic system (8.3% WER from XLS-R) by 61% relative improvement. We provide concrete data requirements and a replicable methodology applicable to other low-resource languages.

</details>


### [496] [Causal Prosody Mediation for Text-to-Speech:Counterfactual Training of Duration, Pitch, and Energy in FastSpeech2](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11683)
*Suvendu Sekhar Mohanty*

Main category: cs.SD

TL;DR: The paper presents a novel causal framework for better controlling prosody and emotion in text-to-speech synthesis, achieving improved expressiveness, naturalness, and manipulation capabilities.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The authors aim to address challenges in disentangling emotional prosody from linguistic content in text-to-speech synthesis, seeking better control over speech expressiveness and naturalness.

Method: The paper proposes augmenting the FastSpeech2 TTS model with explicit emotion conditioning, counterfactual training objectives, and a structural causal model. It introduces novel loss terms to separate emotion and content influences on prosody.

Result: The method outperforms baseline TTS models in prosody manipulation, emotion rendering, and intelligibility while maintaining speaker consistency and naturalness. The results demonstrate high emotion accuracy and better synthesis quality.

Conclusion: The integration of causal principles into TTS enables effective prosody modeling, yielding interpretable and controllable systems capable of generating expressive and natural speech while allowing targeted prosody editing.

Abstract: We propose a novel causal prosody mediation framework for expressive text-to-speech (TTS) synthesis. Our approach augments the FastSpeech2 architecture with explicit emotion conditioning and introduces counterfactual training objectives to disentangle emotional prosody from linguistic content. By formulating a structural causal model of how text (content), emotion, and speaker jointly influence prosody (duration, pitch, energy) and ultimately the speech waveform, we derive two complementary loss terms: an Indirect Path Constraint (IPC) to enforce that emotion affects speech only through prosody, and a Counterfactual Prosody Constraint (CPC) to encourage distinct prosody patterns for different emotions. The resulting model is trained on multi-speaker emotional corpora (LibriTTS, EmoV-DB, VCTK) with a combined objective that includes standard spectrogram reconstruction and variance prediction losses alongside our causal losses. In evaluations on expressive speech synthesis, our method achieves significantly improved prosody manipulation and emotion rendering, with higher mean opinion scores (MOS) and emotion accuracy than baseline FastSpeech2 variants. We also observe better intelligibility (low WER) and speaker consistency when transferring emotions across speakers. Extensive ablations confirm that the causal objectives successfully separate prosody attribution, yielding an interpretable model that allows controlled counterfactual prosody editing (e.g. "same utterance, different emotion") without compromising naturalness. We discuss the implications for identifiability in prosody modeling and outline limitations such as the assumption that emotion effects are fully captured by pitch, duration, and energy. Our work demonstrates how integrating causal learning principles into TTS can improve controllability and expressiveness in generated speech.

</details>


<div id='q-bio.QM'></div>

# q-bio.QM [[Back]](#toc)

### [497] [Ill-Conditioning in Dictionary-Based Dynamic-Equation Learning: A Systems Biology Case Study](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11330)
*Yuxiang Feng,Niall M Mangan,Manu Jayadharan*

Main category: q-bio.QM

TL;DR: This paper analyzes the impact of numerical ill-conditioning on sparse regression approaches in identifying biological dynamics from data, discussing the limitations and possible improvements.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is a need to accurately recover governing equations from time-series data for biological systems, but multicollinearity and numerical instability create challenges in sparse regression methods.

Method: The paper evaluates the effects of strong correlations in candidate function libraries and explores solutions like orthogonal polynomial bases, inspecting their behavior under different data distributions.

Result: It demonstrates that even small candidate terms can cause strong multicollinearity and that orthogonal polynomial bases fail under mismatched data distributions, though aligned data distributions improve performance.

Conclusion: Numerical conditioning is critical for accurate system identification and can be improved by appropriate data sampling and library selection strategies, though challenges remain with strongly correlated terms.

Abstract: Data-driven discovery of governing equations from time-series data provides a powerful framework for understanding complex biological systems. Library-based approaches that use sparse regression over candidate functions have shown considerable promise, but they face a critical challenge when candidate functions become strongly correlated: numerical ill-conditioning. Poor or restricted sampling, together with particular choices of candidate libraries, can produce strong multicollinearity and numerical instability. In such cases, measurement noise may lead to widely different recovered models, obscuring the true underlying dynamics and hindering accurate system identification. Although sparse regularization promotes parsimonious solutions and can partially mitigate conditioning issues, strong correlations may persist, regularization may bias the recovered models, and the regression problem may remain highly sensitive to small perturbations in the data. We present a systematic analysis of how ill-conditioning affects sparse identification of biological dynamics using benchmark models from systems biology. We show that combinations involving as few as two or three terms can already exhibit strong multicollinearity and extremely large condition numbers. We further show that orthogonal polynomial bases do not consistently resolve ill-conditioning and can perform worse than monomial libraries when the data distribution deviates from the weight function associated with the orthogonal basis. Finally, we demonstrate that when data are sampled from distributions aligned with the appropriate weight functions corresponding to the orthogonal basis, numerical conditioning improves, and orthogonal polynomial bases can yield improved model recovery accuracy across two baseline models.

</details>


<div id='cs.MA'></div>

# cs.MA [[Back]](#toc)

### [498] [Enhancing Value Alignment of LLMs with Multi-agent system and Combinatorial Fusion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11126)
*Yuanhong Wu,Djallel Bouneffouf,D. Frank Hsu*

Main category: cs.MA

TL;DR: The paper introduces VAS-CFA, a multi-agent framework leveraging combinatorial fusion to align large language models with diverse human values effectively.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper aims to address the challenge of aligning large language models (LLMs) with human values while acknowledging ethical pluralism which existing methods often neglect.

Method: VAS-CFA involves the use of multiple moral agents, each fine-tuned for distinct normative perspectives, and combines their outputs through combinatorial fusion analysis using rank- and score-based aggregation.

Result: Empirical evaluations show that the VAS-CFA framework improves alignment outcomes compared to single agent approaches and other aggregation methods.

Conclusion: The proposed VAS-CFA framework advances value alignment in LLMs by utilizing cognitive diversity through a robust multi-agent fusion mechanism.

Abstract: Aligning large language models (LLMs) with human values is a central challenge for ensuring trustworthy and safe deployment. While existing methods such as Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) and its variants have improved alignment, they often rely on a single evaluator or narrowly defined reward signals, limiting their ability to capture ethical pluralism. In this work, we propose the Value Alignment System using Combinatorial Fusion Analysis (VAS-CFA), a framework that operationalizes multi-agent fusion alignment. It instantiates multiple moral agents, each fine-tuned to represent a distinct normative perspective, and fuses their outputs using CFA with both rank- and score-based aggregation. This design leverages cognitive diversity, between agents, to mitigate conflicts and redundancies across multiple agents, producing responses that better reflect human values. Empirical evaluation demonstrates that VAS-CFA outperforms both single agent baselines and prior aggregation approaches on standard metrics, showing that multi-agent fusion provides a robust and effective mechanism for advancing value alignment in LLMs.

</details>


### [499] [How Intelligence Emerges: A Minimal Theory of Dynamic Adaptive Coordination](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11560)
*Stefano Grassi*

Main category: cs.MA

TL;DR: This paper introduces a dynamical theory for adaptive coordination in multi-agent systems, focusing on feedback structures involving agents, incentives, and the environment, rather than centralized optimization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper aims to explore adaptive coordination in multi-agent systems beyond equilibrium optimization or purely agent-centered learning, incorporating a feedback architecture among agents, incentives, and environment.

Method: The paper uses a framework that models agents, incentives, and environment as a recursive closed-loop system, analyzing dynamics under dissipativity and persistence assumptions to establish structural results about boundedness, irreducibility, and history sensitivity.

Result: Three key results include: (1) forward-invariant bounded regions under dissipative dynamics; (2) irreducibility of dynamics to static global objectives for persistent environmental memory; and (3) history sensitivity induced by persistent environments unless globally contracting.

Conclusion: The paper concludes that intelligent coordination emerges structurally from the interplay of agents, incentives, and persistent environments, rather than depending on centralized control, welfare optimization, or rational behaviors.

Abstract: This paper develops a dynamical theory of adaptive coordination in multi-agent systems. Rather than analyzing coordination through equilibrium optimization or agent-centric learning alone, the framework models agents, incentives, and environment as a recursively closed feedback architecture. A persistent environment stores accumulated coordination signals, a distributed incentive field transmits those signals locally, and adaptive agents update in response. Coordination is thus treated as a structural property of coupled dynamics rather than as the solution to a centralized objective.
  The paper establishes three structural results. First, under dissipativity assumptions, the induced closed-loop system admits a bounded forward-invariant region, ensuring viability without requiring global optimality. Second, when incentive signals depend non-trivially on persistent environmental memory, the resulting dynamics generically cannot be reduced to a static global objective defined solely over the agent state space. Third, persistent environmental state induces history sensitivity unless the system is globally contracting.
  A minimal linear specification illustrates how coupling, persistence, and dissipation govern local stability and oscillatory regimes through spectral conditions on the Jacobian. The results establish structural conditions under which intelligent coordination dynamics emerge from incentive-mediated adaptive interaction within a persistent environment, without presuming welfare maximization, rational expectations, or centralized design.

</details>


### [500] [Hybrid Human-Agent Social Dilemmas in Energy Markets](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11834)
*Isuri Perera,Frits de Nijs,Julian Garcia*

Main category: cs.MA

TL;DR: The paper studies cooperative behavior emergence in hybrid populations with humans and autonomous agents in energy load management, introducing artificial agents and analyzing their impact under partial adoption.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Exploring how cooperative behaviors can emerge when humans delegate decision-making to autonomous agents, particularly in social dilemmas like energy load management.

Method: Introduced artificial agents using globally observable signals and studied their impact using evolutionary dynamics and reinforcement learning.

Result: Artificial agents can favor coordination; partial adopters aren't penalized, improving outcomes, although asymmetry exists where non-adopters may benefit more.

Conclusion: Partial adoption of AI agents can positively impact coordination, but strategic considerations are crucial due to benefit asymmetry for non-adopters.

Abstract: In hybrid populations where humans delegate strategic decision-making to autonomous agents, understanding when and how cooperative behaviors can emerge remains a key challenge. We study this problem in the context of energy load management: consumer agents schedule their appliance use under demand-dependent pricing. This structure can create a social dilemma where everybody would benefit from coordination, but in equilibrium agents often choose to incur the congestion costs that cooperative turn-taking would avoid. To address the problem of coordination, we introduce artificial agents that use globally observable signals to increase coordination. Using evolutionary dynamics, and reinforcement learning experiments, we show that artificial agents can shift the learning dynamics to favour coordination outcomes. An often neglected problem is partial adoption: what happens when the technology of artificial agents is in the early adoption stages? We analyze mixed populations of adopters and non-adopters, demonstrating that unilateral entry is feasible: adopters are not structurally penalized, and partial adoption can still improve aggregate outcomes. However, in some parameter regimes, non-adopters may benefit disproportionately from the cooperation induced by adopters. This asymmetry, while not precluding beneficial entry, warrants consideration in deployment, and highlights strategic issues around the adoption of AI technology in multiagent settings.

</details>


<div id='cs.SC'></div>

# cs.SC [[Back]](#toc)

### [501] [Interpreting Contrastive Embeddings in Specific Domains with Fuzzy Rules](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.12227)
*Javier Fumanal-Idocin,Mohammadreza Jamalifard,Javier Andreu-Perez*

Main category: cs.SC

TL;DR: The paper focuses on converting free-style text into structured formats using CLIP and fuzzy rule-based classification for specific domains like clinical reports and film reviews, highlighting its limitations and improvements.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome difficulties in analyzing domain-specific free-style text using existing natural language processing tools and to enhance structured representation for machine learning.

Method: Combines fuzzy rule-based classification and text techniques to map features into CLIP model space, analyzing their effectiveness across two domains: clinical reports and film reviews.

Result: Evaluation in clinical reports and film reviews shows results of individual and combined domains, highlighting associations and feature importance.

Conclusion: The approach shows promise but faces limitations; further improvements are necessary for broader application and better domain-specific results.

Abstract: Free-style text is still one of the common ways in which data is registered in real environments, like legal procedures and medical records. Because of that, there have been significant efforts in the area of natural language processing to convert these texts into a structured format, which standard machine learning methods can then exploit. One of the most popular methods to embed text into a vectorial representation is the Contrastive Language-Image Pre-training model (CLIP), which was trained using both image and text. Although the representations computed by CLIP have been very successful in zero-show and few-shot learning problems, they still have problems when applied to a particular domain. In this work, we use a fuzzy rule-based classification system along with some standard text procedure techniques to map some of our features of interest to the space created by a CLIP model. Then, we discuss the rules and associations obtained and the importance of each feature considered. We apply this approach in two different data domains, clinical reports and film reviews, and compare the results obtained individually and when considering both. Finally, we discuss the limitations of this approach and how it could be further improved.

</details>


<div id='cs.NI'></div>

# cs.NI [[Back]](#toc)

### [502] [Agentic AI for Embodied-enhanced Beam Prediction in Low-Altitude Economy Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11392)
*Min Hao,Zhizhuo Li,Zirui Zhang,Maoqiang Wu,Han Zhang,Rong Yu*

Main category: cs.NI

TL;DR: The paper proposes a multi-agent collaborative reasoning architecture and a hybrid beam prediction model for UAV-to-ground mmWave communication to address challenges of propagation loss and beam directivity in high mobility scenarios, achieving high accuracy in predictions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The study is driven by the necessity to improve beam prediction for unstable mmWave or terahertz communications in low-altitude UAV networks, which are crucial for real-time and high-throughput operations.

Method: The authors developed a multi-agent reasoning architecture that breaks the prediction task into smaller parts and a hybrid beam model for processing multimodal UAV data, combining numerical mobility information and visual data with advanced temporal, visual, and cross-attention modeling.

Result: The proposed solution demonstrates high prediction accuracy and robustness, achieving a top-1 accuracy of up to 96.57% in simulations using a real UAV mmWave communication dataset.

Conclusion: The integration of multi-agent reasoning and a hybrid beam prediction model system paves the way for efficient UAV-based mmWave communications, offering reliable solutions for real-world mobility and data diversity challenges.

Abstract: Millimeter-wave or terahertz communications can meet demands of low-altitude economy networks for high-throughput sensing and real-time decision making. However, high-frequency characteristics of wireless channels result in severe propagation loss and strong beam directivity, which make beam prediction challenging in highly mobile uncrewed aerial vehicles (UAV) scenarios. In this paper, we employ agentic AI to enable the transformation of mmWave base stations toward embodied intelligence. We innovatively design a multi-agent collaborative reasoning architecture for UAV-to-ground mmWave communications and propose a hybrid beam prediction model system based on bimodal data. The multi-agent architecture is designed to overcome the limited context window and weak controllability of large language model (LLM)-based reasoning by decomposing beam prediction into task analysis, solution planning, and completeness assessment. To align with the agentic reasoning process, a hybrid beam prediction model system is developed to process multimodal UAV data, including numeric mobility information and visual observations. The proposed hybrid model system integrates Mamba-based temporal modelling, convolutional visual encoding, and cross-attention-based multimodal fusion, and dynamically switches data-flow strategies under multi-agent guidance. Extensive simulations on a real UAV mmWave communication dataset demonstrate that proposed architecture and system achieve high prediction accuracy and robustness under diverse data conditions, with maximum top-1 accuracy reaching 96.57%.

</details>


### [503] [Efficient Cross-View Localization in 6G Space-Air-Ground Integrated Network](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11398)
*Min Hao,Yanbing Xu,Maoqiang Wu,Jinglin Huang,Chen Shang,Jiacheng Wang,Ruichen Zhang,Jiawen Kang,Dusit Niyato,Zhu Han,Wei Ni*

Main category: cs.NI

TL;DR: This paper integrates cross-view localization (CVL) with the 6G space-air-ground integrated network (SAGIN) to improve performance in terms of latency, energy efficiency, and privacy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper aims to enhance localization reliability by integrating CVL with the emerging 6G SAGIN architecture to overcome existing challenges of latency, energy consumption, and privacy issues.

Method: The authors propose a split-inference framework for CVL, leveraging distributed communication and computing resources in the 6G SAGIN architecture. They also perform joint optimization on communication, computing, and confidentiality.

Result: The proposed framework is validated through experiments, showing improvements in localization accuracy, processing speed, and solutions to the optimization problems of communication, computation, and privacy.

Conclusion: Integrating CVL with 6G SAGIN offers significant advantages in localization performance, and the proposed framework provides a direction for efficient implementations while suggesting potential future research areas.

Abstract: Recently, visual localization has become an important supplement to improve localization reliability, and cross-view approaches can greatly enhance coverage and adaptability. Meanwhile, future 6G will enable a globally covered mobile communication system, with a space-air-ground integrated network (SAGIN) serving as key supporting architecture. Inspired by this, we explore an integration of cross-view localization (CVL) with 6G SAGIN, thereby enhancing its performance in latency, energy consumption, and privacy protection. First, we provide a comprehensive review of CVL and SAGIN, highlighting their capabilities, integration opportunities, and potential applications. Benefiting from the fast and extensive image collection and transmission capabilities of the 6G SAGIN architecture, CVL achieves higher localization accuracy and faster processing speed. Then, we propose a split-inference framework for implementing CVL, which fully leverages the distributed communication and computing resources of the 6G SAGIN architecture. Subsequently, we conduct joint optimization of communication, computation, and confidentiality within the proposed split-inference framework, aiming to provide a paradigm and a direction for making CVL efficient. Experimental results validate the effectiveness of the proposed framework and provide solutions to the optimization problem. Finally, we discuss potential research directions for 6G SAGIN-enabled CVL.

</details>


<div id='eess.AS'></div>

# eess.AS [[Back]](#toc)

### [504] [Cough activity detection for automatic tuberculosis screening](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11241)
*Joshua Jansen van Vüren,Devendra Singh Parihar,Daphne Naidoo,Kimsey Zajac,Willy Ssengooba,Grant Theron,Thomas Niesler*

Main category: eess.AS

TL;DR: The paper presents a method for automatic cough detection in audio files using pre-trained models, achieving high precision and efficiency for health screening in pulmonary diseases.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to enable reliable and scalable solutions for health screening, particularly in identifying cases of pulmonary diseases such as tuberculosis (TB), by detecting cough start and end points in audio recordings.

Method: The method uses pre-trained architectures, particularly the XLS-R model, for cough activity detection in an audio dataset collected from TB patients in South Africa and Uganda. Comparisons are made with other models like AST and logistic regression.

Result: The XLS-R model achieved an average precision of 0.96 and an AUC of 0.99 for cough detection. The best results were achievable using only the first three layers of the XLS-R model, making it efficient for smartphone applications.

Conclusion: Pre-trained transformer models like XLS-R are effective for detecting cough segments in audio with high precision, making them suitable for integration into scalable health screening tools.

Abstract: The automatic identification of cough segments in audio through the determination of start and end points is pivotal to building scalable screening tools in health technologies for pulmonary related diseases. We propose the application of two current pre-trained architectures to the task of cough activity detection. A dataset of recordings containing cough from patients symptomatic for tuberculosis (TB) who self-present at community-level care centres in South Africa and Uganda is employed. When automatic start and end points are determined using XLS-R, an average precision of 0.96 and an area under the receiver-operating characteristic of 0.99 are achieved for the test set. We show that best average precision is achieved by utilising only the first three layers of the network, which has the dual benefits of reduced computational and memory requirements, pivotal for smartphone-based applications. This XLS-R configuration is shown to outperform an audio spectrogram transformer (AST) as well as a logistic regression baseline by 9% and 27% absolute in test set average precision respectively. Furthermore, a downstream TB classification model trained using the coughs automatically isolated by XLS-R comfortably outperforms a model trained on the coughs isolated by AST, and is only narrowly outperformed by a classifier trained on the ground truth coughs. We conclude that the application of large pre-trained transformer models is an effective approach to identifying cough end-points and that the integration of such a model into a screening tool is feasible.

</details>


### [505] [Affect Decoding in Phonated and Silent Speech Production from Surface EMG](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11715)
*Simon Pistrosch,Kleanthis Avramidis,Tiantian Feng,Jihwan Lee,Monica Gonzalez-Machorro,Shrikanth Narayanan,Björn W. Schuller*

Main category: eess.AS

TL;DR: The study explores how surface electromyography (sEMG) captures emotional expressions during speech (both phonated and silent) and demonstrates its ability to decode emotions reliably, especially frustration, using a novel dataset.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to investigate the link between emotion and articulatory muscle activity during spoken communication, aiming to enhance understanding of how emotions modulate speech and potentially apply this knowledge to develop emotion-aware silent speech technologies.

Method: The study utilized a dataset of 2,780 utterances from 12 participants performing three tasks, capturing facial and neck EMG signals during phonated and silent speech. Various features and models were evaluated to decode affect both within and across participants.

Result: The findings show that EMG representations effectively decode frustration with an AUC of up to 0.845 and generalize well across different speech articulation modes. Affective signals were identified even in the absence of phonation.

Conclusion: The study demonstrates that facial motor activity encodes affective information, which persists even without phonation, indicating the potential for EMG-based emotion decoding in silent speech scenarios.

Abstract: The expression of affect is integral to spoken communication, yet, its link to underlying articulatory execution remains unclear. Measures of articulatory muscle activity such as EMG could reveal how speech production is modulated by emotion alongside acoustic speech analyses. We investigate affect decoding from facial and neck surface electromyography (sEMG) during phonated and silent speech production. For this purpose, we introduce a dataset comprising 2,780 utterances from 12 participants across 3 tasks, on which we evaluate both intra- and inter-subject decoding using a range of features and model embeddings. Our results reveal that EMG representations reliably discriminate frustration with up to 0.845 AUC, and generalize well across articulation modes. Our ablation study further demonstrates that affective signatures are embedded in facial motor activity and persist in the absence of phonation, highlighting the potential of EMG sensing for affect-aware silent speech interfaces.

</details>


### [506] [Dr. SHAP-AV: Decoding Relative Modality Contributions via Shapley Attribution in Audio-Visual Speech Recognition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.12046)
*Umberto Cappellazzo,Stavros Petridis,Maja Pantic*

Main category: eess.AS

TL;DR: The paper introduces Dr. SHAP-AV, a framework utilizing Shapley values to analyze contributions from acoustic and visual modalities in audio-visual speech recognition.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Understanding how models balance acoustic and visual contributions for robust speech recognition under noisy environments.

Method: Dr. SHAP-AV leverages Shapley values with three analytical approaches: Global SHAP for modality balance, Generative SHAP for decoding dynamics, and Temporal Alignment SHAP for input-output correspondence.

Result: Experiments show models shift reliance toward visual contributions under noise but maintain significant audio influence, with SNR level driving modality weighting.

Conclusion: Findings reveal audio bias persists in AVSR, motivating mechanisms for improved modality weighting and introducing Shapley-based attribution as a diagnostic standard.

Abstract: Audio-Visual Speech Recognition (AVSR) leverages both acoustic and visual information for robust recognition under noise. However, how models balance these modalities remains unclear. We present Dr. SHAP-AV, a framework using Shapley values to analyze modality contributions in AVSR. Through experiments on six models across two benchmarks and varying SNR levels, we introduce three analyses: Global SHAP for overall modality balance, Generative SHAP for contribution dynamics during decoding, and Temporal Alignment SHAP for input-output correspondence. Our findings reveal that models shift toward visual reliance under noise yet maintain high audio contributions even under severe degradation. Modality balance evolves during generation, temporal alignment holds under noise, and SNR is the dominant factor driving modality weighting. These findings expose a persistent audio bias, motivating ad-hoc modality-weighting mechanisms and Shapley-based attribution as a standard AVSR diagnostic.

</details>


<div id='math.DS'></div>

# math.DS [[Back]](#toc)

### [507] [Exploring Collatz Dynamics with Human-LLM Collaboration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11066)
*Edward Y. Chang*

Main category: math.DS

TL;DR: This paper investigates structural properties of the Collatz iteration, offering modular and trajectory-based insights to suggest conditional convergence frameworks, though key hypotheses are still open.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To better understand the structural behaviors and convergence of the Collatz iteration by exploring computational and theoretical phenomena.

Method: The authors use large computational exploration and theoretical proofs to identify modular scrambling and trajectory-based dynamics, including lemmas describing bijections, gap structure, geometric distributions, and orbit contraction.

Result: They establish formulas and properties for modular scrambling, gap dynamics, and statistical distributions, leading to a predictive framework for convergence under specific hypotheses.

Conclusion: The findings contribute to a conditional framework for Collatz convergence with open hypotheses, highlighting new avenues for exploring this longstanding mathematical problem.

Abstract: We investigate structural properties of the Collatz iteration through two phenomena observed in large computational exploration: modular scrambling of residue classes and a burst--gap decomposition of trajectories. We prove several structural results, including a modular scrambling lemma showing that the gap-return map acts as an exact bijection on high bits, a persistent exit lemma characterizing gap structure after persistent states, and a decay property for known portions of binary representations under gap-return dynamics. We further prove that, in the modular model, gap lengths and $2$-adic valuations follow geometric distributions, while persistent run lengths are geometric with expected burst length $E[B]=2$; together these predict strict orbit contraction. These results suggest a conditional framework in which convergence would follow from suitable orbitwise hypotheses on burst and gap lengths, which in turn are suggested by an orbit equidistribution conjecture. However, the key hypotheses remain open, and the framework is exploratory rather than a complete reduction. The paper also documents the human-LLM collaboration through which these observations were developed.

</details>


<div id='q-bio.GN'></div>

# q-bio.GN [[Back]](#toc)

### [508] [A Standardized Framework For Evaluating Gene Expression Generative Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11244)
*Andrea Rubbi,Andrea Giuseppe Di Francesco,Mohammad Lotfollahi,Pietro Liò*

Main category: q-bio.GN

TL;DR: The paper presents GGE, a Python framework for evaluating generative models for single-cell gene expression data, addressing inconsistencies in metrics, hyperparameters, and biologically-grounded evaluation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Generative models for single-cell data are advancing rapidly, but evaluation practices lack standardization, inhibiting fair comparison and benchmarking.

Method: The authors created GGE, a Python-based framework, providing distributional metrics, DEG-focused evaluations, perturbation-effect correlation, and standardized computation space options.

Result: GGE demonstrates metric inconsistencies in the field, identifies critical standardization needs, and enables fair comparisons of models through comprehensive evaluation capabilities.

Conclusion: GGE is a valuable tool to unify and standardize evaluation protocols, ultimately accelerating research in cellular modeling and inference scenarios.

Abstract: The rapid development of generative models for single-cell gene expression data has created an urgent need for standardised evaluation frameworks. Current evaluation practices suffer from inconsistent metric implementations, incomparable hyperparameter choices, and a lack of biologically-grounded metrics. We present Generated Genetic Expression Evaluator (GGE), an open-source Python framework that addresses these challenges by providing a comprehensive suite of distributional metrics with explicit computation space options and biologically-motivated evaluation through differentially expressed gene (DEG)-focused analysis and perturbation-effect correlation, enabling standardized reporting and reproducible benchmarking. Through extensive analysis of the single-cell generative modeling literature, we identify that no standardized evaluation protocol exists. Methods report incomparable metrics computed in different spaces with different hyperparameters. We demonstrate that metric values vary substantially depending on implementation choices, highlighting the critical need for standardization.
